retrieved_docs.title: Nephrotic Syndrome -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: There are different types of glomerulonephritis causing nephrotic syndrome, and they all behave differently when it comes to histopathological features of the kidney biopsy.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Nephron -- Clinical Significance -- Nephritic Syndrome
retrieved_docs.content: As with nephrotic syndrome, nephritic syndromes can also be primary or secondary. Some of the most common causes of nephritic syndrome are post-infectious glomerulonephritis, IgA nephropathy, and lupus nephritis. Poststreptococcal nephritic syndrome arises 2 to 3 weeks after group A B-hemolytic nephritogenic streptococcal infection of the skin (impetigo) or pharynx. It usually occurs in children but can also occur in adults. No characteristic finding on H&E stain but granular IF pattern and subepithelial immune complex deposition (“humps”) on EM. [24] IgA is the most common nephropathy worldwide. It is characterized by the IgA immune complex deposition in the mesangium of glomeruli. It commonly presents as hematuria following mucosal infections, especially gastroenteritis. [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Glomerulonephritis -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Primary glomerulonephritis presenting as the nephrotic syndrome in young patients is likely to be minimal change disease, while in adults, membranous variety is more likely. [45] In the secondary category, diabetes mellitus has to be ruled out.

retrieved_docs.title: Nephrotic Syndrome -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The most common cause in children is minimal change glomerulonephritis. In White adults, nephrotic syndrome is most frequently due to membranous nephropathy, whereas in populations of African ancestry, the most common cause of the nephrotic syndrome is focal segmental glomerulosclerosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Glomerulonephritis -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Based on the clinical presentation, differentiation needs to be drawn between the nephrotic and the nephritic spectrum. This is important as it helps to narrow down the differentials of the underlying glomerular pathology. Also, differential diagnoses will include primary versus secondary causes depending on the age group and clinical picture.

retrieved_docs.title: Glomerulonephritis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Etiological classification of glomerulonephritis can be made based on clinical presentation, ranging from severe proteinuria (>3.5 g/day) and edema qualifying for the nephrotic syndrome to a nephritic syndrome where hematuria and hypertension are more prominent while proteinuria is less pronounced.

retrieved_docs.title: Nephrotic Syndrome -- Epidemiology -- International Statistics
retrieved_docs.content: In India and Turkey, biopsy results in children with nephrotic syndrome have revealed similar histology types compared to what would be expected in Western countries. [14] [15] In Pakistani adult patients with nephrotic syndrome, the histological patterns of kidney biopsies are similar to those seen in western countries. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Kidney and Glomerulus -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Glomerular diseases are heterogeneous conditions with a wide variety of etiologies and clinical presentations, but almost universally are due to disruption of the glomerular filtration barrier. These different kidney diseases can broadly separate into two syndromes based on the number of proteins passing into the filtrate or glomerular filtration rate: nephrotic and nephritic. [2] These syndromes are not diseases in themselves, but a set of symptoms, signs, and laboratory findings that suggest an underlying kidney disorder most often involving the glomerulus. While the etiologies and pathologic changes that lead to nephrotic and nephritic syndromes are highly variable, some useful generalizations are possible.

retrieved_docs.title: Nephrotic Syndrome -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Common primary causes of nephrotic syndrome are intrinsic kidney diseases, such as membranous nephropathy, minimal-­change nephropathy, and focal glomerulosclerosis. Secondary causes may include systemic diseases, such as lupus erythematosus, diabetes mellitus, and amyloidosis. Congenital/hereditary focal glomerulosclerosis could occur because of genetic mutations in podocyte proteins, such as podocin, nephrin, or the cation channel 6 protein. [5] An episode of infectious diseases, particularly the upper respiratory tract, is a triggering factor in almost half of cases, an allergic reaction in a third of cases, and more rarely, an insect bite or vaccination. [6] Nephrotic syndrome can also result from drugs of abuse, including heroin. [7] Secondary causes of nephrotic syndrome include the following:

retrieved_docs.title: Congenital Nephrotic Syndrome -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The etiology of congenital nephrotic syndrome is heterogeneous. The etiology can be divided into genetic, non-genetic, and infectious.

retrieved_docs.title: Nephrotic Syndrome -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The nephrotic syndrome could affect adults and children of both genders and any race. Also, it could occur in a typical form or with nephritic syndrome. The latter denotes glomerular inflammation leading to hematuria and impaired renal function.

retrieved_docs.title: Nephrotic Syndrome -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Membranous glomerulonephritis: Just 2% to 4% of cases in children, but the most common type in adults. Thickened basement membrane and granular pattern on immunofluorescence. A characteristic “spike and dome” appearance is visible on electron microscopy, with membrane deposition growing around subepithelial immune complex deposition. [33]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Nephron -- Clinical Significance -- Nephrotic Syndrome
retrieved_docs.content: Nephrotic syndrome may be primary or secondary. Primary nephrotic syndrome occurs when the kidney is the primary or sole affected organ. Secondary nephrotic syndrome is when systemic immunologic, metabolic, or vascular diseases affect the glomeruli. Out of these two, secondary nephrotic syndrome is the most common. [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Nephrotic Syndrome -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis: More commonly presents as nephrotic syndrome. It involves immune complex deposition. Immunofluorescence staining shows a granular pattern. On light microscopy, one can see thickened basement membrane. [32]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Nephron -- Clinical Significance -- Primary Nephrotic Syndrome
retrieved_docs.content: The following are some of the most common causes of primary nephrotic syndrome.

retrieved_docs.title: Congenital Nephrotic Syndrome -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The Finnish type's genetic form of congenital nephrotic syndrome can be divided as autosomal dominant or  (AD) autosomal recessive (AR). [3]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2508
retrieved_docs.content: Urinary tract disorder (اضطراب السبيل البولي) encompasses a broad range of conditions affecting the urinary system, and within this category lies kidney failure (قصور الكلى). Kidney failure functions as a parent condition for specific renal diseases, including nephritis, nephrosis, and nephrotic syndrome. Understanding that nephritis, nephrosis, and nephrotic syndrome are subtypes of kidney failure, which itself is a subset of urinary tract disorders, provides clinicians with a hierarchical framework for diagnosing, classifying, and managing these interrelated kidney and urinary system conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2925
retrieved_docs.content: Chronic kidney disease is a broad renal disorder that encompasses a spectrum of conditions including nephritis, nephrosis, and nephrotic syndrome. When elevated blood pressure causes progressive renal damage, the resulting condition is identified as chronic kidney disease due to hypertension, which is a specific subtype of chronic kidney disease. Recognizing that chronic kidney disease can arise from hypertension and that it includes various glomerular diseases such as nephritis, nephrosis, and nephrotic syndrome guides clinicians to prioritize blood‑pressure control and tailored management of the underlying renal pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 2447
retrieved_docs.content: Necrotizing glomerulonephritis is classified as a form of اعتلال كبيبات الكلى (glomerular disease) and is frequently encountered as a component of متلازمة رئوية كلوية (pulmonary‑renal syndrome). As a severe, necrotizing inflammation of the renal glomeruli, it represents a specific subtype within the broader category of glomerular pathology. Its association with pulmonary‑renal syndrome highlights the clinical pattern in which simultaneous lung hemorrhage and rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis occur, often due to small‑vessel vasculitis or autoimmune mechanisms. Recognizing necrotizing glomerulonephritis as both a distinct glomerular disease and a manifestation of pulmonary‑renal syndrome is essential for accurate diagnosis, prompt immunosuppressive treatment, and coordinated care of the intertwined renal and pulmonary complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 3 -- Community 2737
retrieved_docs.content: GLOMERULONEFRITIA/NEFROSIA is identified as the English equivalent of التهاب كبيبات الكلى الحاد, an acute inflammatory condition of the kidney glomeruli. This acute glomerular disease is situated within the broader category of اضطراب السبيل البولي, indicating that it is one of the many disorders affecting the urinary tract. Additionally, التهاب كبيبات الكلى الحاد is classified under the parent grouping of nephritis, nephrosis, and nephrotic syndrome, linking it to the larger spectrum of renal inflammatory and protein‑leakage disorders. Together, these relationships highlight that GLOMERULONEFRITIA/NEFROSIA (acute glomerulonephritis) is both a specific urinary system pathology and a component of the broader renal disease classifications, underscoring its relevance for diagnosis, management, and understanding of kidney‑related health conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 2 -- Community 370
retrieved_docs.content: nephrosis, and nephrotic syndrome” group is shown to have acute glomerulonephritis as a parent condition, underscoring the progression from acute inflammatory injury to chronic nephrotic states. Overall, these interconnected classifications emphasize that CKD resides within a network of renal pathologies ranging from acute glomerular inflammation to systemic immune‑mediated diseases such as lupus nephritis and thrombotic microangiopathies like hemolytic uremic syndrome. Recognizing these relationships aids clinicians in understanding disease progression, overlapping diagnostic categories, and the importance of integrated management across acute and chronic kidney disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 726
retrieved_docs.content: Nephritis; nephrosis; renal sclerosis are glomerular disorders that manifest in the glomerulus and are included as members of متلازمة التفاعل الدوائي المترافق مع كثرة اليوزينيات والأعراض الجهازية (SMQ). These conditions are conceptually linked to التهاب كبيبات الكلى التكاثري (proliferative glomerulonephritis), which is recognized as a subtype of nephritis; nephrosis; renal sclerosis and is also a member of الذئبة الحمامية المجموعية (SMQ), reflecting its role in systemic lupus erythematosus–related kidney involvement. Furthermore, nephritis; nephrosis; renal sclerosis are mapped to التهاب كبيبات الكلى بعد الالتهاب العقدي (post‑infectious glomerulonephritis), indicating a correspondence between these renal pathologies and the immune‑mediated response that follows certain infections. Together, these relationships highlight how various forms of glomerular inflammation, sclerosis, and nephrotic processes intersect within drug‑induced syndromes, autoimmune disease clusters, and post‑infectious contexts, underscoring the importance of recognizing shared pathogenic pathways for accurate diagnosis and comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2177
retrieved_docs.content: Necrotizing glomerulonephritis is a severe, necrotizing form of التهاب كبيبات الكلى (glomerulonephritis), representing a specific subtype within the broader category of glomerular inflammation. This disease is closely associated with متلازمة رئوية كلوية (pulmonary‑renal syndrome), a clinical entity in which acute lung involvement—often pulmonary hemorrhage—occurs together with rapid deterioration of renal function. Understanding that necrotizing glomerulonephritis both belongs to the spectrum of glomerulonephritis and can manifest as part of a pulmonary‑renal syndrome is essential for timely diagnosis, comprehensive evaluation of both kidney and lung pathology, and the implementation of appropriate immunosuppressive and supportive therapies to improve patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 4 -- Community 2919
retrieved_docs.content: GLOMERULONEFRITIA/NEFROSIA is identified as the English equivalent of the Arabic term التهاب كبيبات الكلى الحاد, which describes acute inflammation of the kidney glomeruli. This specific condition is classified within the broader parent category of nephritis, nephrosis, and nephrotic syndrome, indicating that acute glomerular inflammation is considered a type of renal disease encompassed by these related disorders. Recognizing GLOMERULONEFRITIA/NEFROSIA as a subset of nephritis, nephrosis, and nephrotic syndrome helps integrate terminology across languages and situates the condition within the larger framework of kidney pathology for clinical assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 740
retrieved_docs.content: central concept linked to hypertension, specific renal syndromes (hemolytic‑uremic syndrome, lupus nephritis, crescentic glomerulonephritis), procedural interventions, and broader interpretations of impairment, emphasizing the importance of integrated diagnostic coding, etiologic understanding, and multidisciplinary management in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 917
retrieved_docs.content: Necrotizing glomerulonephritis is closely linked to anti‑basement membrane disease, a condition that manifests as renal dysfunction, and it is also a key component of pulmonary‑renal syndrome. This form of glomerular injury encompasses the entities “nephritis; nephrosis; renal sclerosis,” which are localized to the glomerulus and represent specific types of proliferative glomerulonephritis. These three entities are further mapped to post‑infectious glomerulonephritis, indicating that they share pathological features with glomerular inflammation that follows an infectious episode. Proliferative glomerulonephritis, the broader category that includes “nephritis; nephrosis; renal sclerosis,” also contains systemic lupus erythematosus (SMQ) as a member, highlighting the autoimmune contribution to this pattern of kidney disease. Together, these relationships illustrate how necrotizing glomerulonephritis, anti‑basement membrane disease, pulmonary‑renal syndrome, post‑infectious glomerulonephritis, and systemic lupus erythematosus are interconnected through shared glomerular pathology and renal functional impairment, underscoring the importance of integrated diagnostic and therapeutic approaches in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 1248
retrieved_docs.content: Necrotizing glomerulonephritis is classified under the broader category of اعتلال كبيبات الكلى (renal glomerular disease). It is closely associated with داء مضاد للغشاء القاعدي الكبيبي (anti‑basement membrane disease), reflecting an immunologic mechanism that targets the glomerular basement membrane. Clinically, necrotizing glomerulonephritis often occurs within متلازمة رئوية كلوية (pulmonary‑renal syndrome), linking severe renal inflammation with pulmonary hemorrhage. Understanding these interconnections is essential for accurate diagnosis and coordinated management of patients who present with combined kidney and lung involvement.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 1810
retrieved_docs.content: Necrotizing glomerulonephritis is a severe, necrotizing form of التهاب كبيبات الكلى (glomerulonephritis) that is classified under اعتلال كبيبات الكلى (renal glomerular disease). It is closely associated with داء مضاد للغشاء القاعدي الكبيبي (anti‑glomerular basement membrane disease), a condition that can trigger the immune‑mediated injury of the glomerular basement membrane. Clinically, necrotizing glomerulonephritis often presents as متلازمة رئوية كلوية (pulmonary‑renal syndrome), reflecting simultaneous involvement of the lungs and kidneys. Within the broader category of اعتلال كبيبات الكلى, other serious sequelae such as اليوريمية (uremia) are also recognized as child conditions, underscoring the potential for progressive renal dysfunction when glomerular pathology is present. Understanding these interrelationships is essential for accurate diagnosis, targeted therapy, and comprehensive management of patients with necrotizing glomerulonephritis and related glomerular disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 2503
retrieved_docs.content: Necrotizing glomerulonephritis is a specific, severe form of التهاب كبيبات الكلى (glomerulonephritis) and is directly linked to متلازمة رئوية كلوية (pulmonary‑renal syndrome). In the broader context, التهاب كبيبات الكلى is clinically associated with قصور كلوي مزمن (chronic renal failure), indicating that patients with this type of glomerular inflammation are at risk for progressive loss of kidney function. Recognizing the relationship between necrotizing glomerulonephritis, pulmonary‑renal syndrome, and the potential development of chronic renal failure is essential for timely diagnosis, targeted therapy, and comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2623
retrieved_docs.content: Necrotizing glomerulonephritis is closely linked to داء مضاد للغشاء القاعدي الكبيبي, an anti‑basement membrane disease of the kidney, and it also forms a key component of متلازمة رئية كلوية, a pulmonary‑renal syndrome that involves simultaneous injury to the lungs and kidneys. This interrelationship highlights the importance of recognizing necrotizing glomerulonephritis not only as a severe renal pathology but also as a manifestation of systemic autoimmune processes that can affect multiple organ systems, guiding comprehensive diagnostic evaluation and coordinated therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2463
retrieved_docs.content: Chronic kidney disease is a renal disorder that falls within the broader category of nephritis, nephrosis, and nephrotic syndrome, indicating its relationship to inflammatory and protein‑losing kidney conditions. A specific form of this disease, chronic kidney disease due to hypertension, is recognized as a subtype of chronic kidney disease, highlighting the role of sustained high blood pressure as an underlying cause. Among the clinical manifestations of chronic kidney disease is عدم تحمل بروتين اللايسين, an intolerance to leucine‑containing proteins, which can contribute to the disease’s symptom profile and influence dietary management strategies. Understanding chronic kidney disease’s classification, its hypertensive subtype, and its protein intolerance manifestation is essential for accurate diagnosis, targeted treatment, and comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 1438
retrieved_docs.content: Chronic kidney disease is a progressive renal condition that can arise from multiple specific etiologies. One recognized form is chronic kidney disease due to hypertension, which is classified as a subtype of chronic kidney disease. Another condition that is mapped to chronic kidney disease is glomerulonephritis/nephrosis (GLOMERULONEFRITIA/NEFROSIA), indicating that these inflammatory and scarring kidney disorders are considered manifestations of chronic kidney disease. In clinical data collection, the status of chronic kidney disease may also be recorded as “Unknown,” which is an accepted permissible value when the presence or cause of the disease cannot be determined. Recognizing these subtypes and permissible reporting options is important for accurate diagnosis, documentation, and management of patients with chronic kidney disease.
